title: Recursion
summary: Solve problems by having functions call themselves with simpler inputs

sections.title: Recursion is a function that calls itself
sections.content: Imagine standing between two mirrors — you see an infinite reflection of yourself, each one smaller than the last. Recursion works similarly: a function solves a big problem by breaking it into smaller versions of the same problem, calling itself with progressively simpler inputs until reaching a base case (the smallest version that has an immediate answer). Every recursive function needs two parts: the base case that stops the recursion (like 'if there's only one item, return it'), and the recursive case that moves toward the base case. Without a base case, the function calls itself forever — just like mirrors facing each other.

sections.title: Calculating factorial with recursion
sections.content: Factorial (5!) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1 — solved by reducing the problem step by step

sections.title: When to use recursion vs iteration
sections.content: Recursion shines for problems with a naturally recursive structure: tree traversal (file systems, HTML DOM), divide-and-conquer algorithms (quick sort, merge sort), and mathematical sequences (factorial, Fibonacci). The code is often shorter and more elegant than iterative alternatives. However, recursion has costs: each call consumes stack memory, and Python has a default recursion limit (1000). Deep recursion hits this limit, causing RecursionError. Iteration (loops) uses constant stack space and is usually faster. Use recursion when the recursive solution is clearly simpler; use iteration when performance matters or depth is unpredictable.

sections.title: Forgetting the base case causes RecursionError
sections.content: Without a base case, recursion never stops and eventually hits Python's recursion limit. Wrong: def countdown(n): print(n); countdown(n-1) — infinite recursion! Correct: add if n <= 0: return before the recursive call. Always define the base case first and make sure every recursive call moves toward it.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write a recursive function called sum_digits(n) that takes a positive integer and returns the sum of its digits. For example, sum_digits(123) should return 1 + 2 + 3 = 6. Hint: use the modulo operator (%) to get the last digit and integer division (//) to remove the last digit in the recursive call.
hints: What is the base case — when should the recursion stop?
How can you extract the last digit and the remaining number separately?
